subject: Physics
title: Thunder: Sound of Lightning
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Thunder: Sound of Lightning.

hook.text: Lightning heats the air to 30,000°C in a fraction of a second. That superheated air explodes—creating thunder.

buildup.text: The lightning bolt is only a few centimeters wide, but it heats air five times hotter than the Sun's surface.

discovery.text: The rapid expansion of air creates a shock wave. That shock wave reaches your ears as a boom.

twist.text: Thunder rumbles because the lightning bolt is long. Sound from different parts arrives at different times.

climax.text: Light travels instantly; sound takes 3 seconds per kilometer. Count the gap to estimate the storm's distance.

punchline.text: Lightning superheats air. Air explodes. You hear the explosion as thunder.

quiz.question: Why does thunder rumble rather than produce a single sharp crack?
options: Sound from different parts of the long bolt arrives at different times
The sound echoes off multiple cloud layers
Thunder naturally oscillates at low frequencies
